serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore restaurant plate of Fettuccine Alfredo weighing approximately 350g, as typically served at Italian restaurants and Western cafés in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fettuccine Alfredo — Component Calculation using USDA SR Legacy ingredient data (fettuccine pasta, butter, double cream, parmesan, black pepper)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA SR Legacy ingredient entries for cooked fettuccine pasta, unsalted butter, double cream (heavy whipping cream), parmesan cheese, and black pepper, combined in proportions consistent with a Singapore restaurant-style Alfredo plate (~350g). Per-100g values represent the blended composite. Confidence: medium, as exact restaurant formulation varies.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fettuccine / pasta al dente analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database values for white fettuccine cooked al dente (GI range 32–57, central estimate ~52). The high fat content of the Alfredo sauce (butter and double cream) further slows gastric emptying, which may modestly reduce the effective GI relative to plain pasta; however, the published GI for fettuccine al dente is used as the primary reference. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (52 × 65g) / 100 ≈ 33.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (~700mg per plate) and high saturated fat contributing to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk
hypertension.guidance: At ~700mg sodium per 350g plate, Fettuccine Alfredo is lower in sodium than cured-meat pasta dishes, but the 30g saturated fat per plate elevates LDL cholesterol, which independently increases cardiovascular risk in hypertensive patients with metabolic syndrome; limit to occasional consumption and pair with a low-sodium meal context.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~34 per plate) from pasta carbohydrate, with prolonged postprandial glucose elevation due to fat-slowed gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: The GL of ~34 per plate exceeds the high-GL threshold; the very high fat content (~48g) from cream and butter slows gastric emptying, producing a broad, sustained postprandial glucose elevation rather than a sharp spike — which remains clinically significant for HbA1c management; T2DM patients should limit portion to half a plate and consider adding a lean protein source to further moderate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 30g per plate (150% of WHO daily limit of 20g), driving LDL elevation and atherosclerotic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A single 350g plate of Fettuccine Alfredo delivers approximately 30g of saturated fat — 150% of the WHO recommended daily maximum — primarily from double cream and butter; patients with established CVD or elevated LDL should avoid this dish or consume it very rarely (no more than once monthly), as regular consumption is incompatible with a cardioprotective dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: 30g saturated fat per plate — highest of any pasta dish in this batch — associated with elevated systemic inflammation markers (CRP, IL-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients following an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern should strongly limit or avoid Fettuccine Alfredo; the 30g saturated fat per plate from dairy cream and butter is the primary anti-inflammatory diet transgression, and if pasta is desired, a tomato-based or lean-meat sauce (e.g., Bolognese at ~7g sat fat) is substantially more compatible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fettuccine Alfredo — Singapore Restaurant Style is a rich Italian-inspired pasta dish served as a main course at Italian restaurants and Western cafés across Singapore. A standard restaurant plate weighs approximately 350g and delivers approximately 820 kcal, making it the most energy-dense of common pasta dishes served in Singapore. The dish consists of wide flat fettuccine pasta coated in a sauce made from unsalted butter, double cream (35–40% fat), finely grated parmesan cheese, and black pepper. The Singapore restaurant version is notably richer than the original Roman preparation (which used only butter and pasta water), as double cream is incorporated to produce a thicker, more indulgent sauce. Texture is silky and coating; flavour is intensely buttery, mildly salty, and savoury from the parmesan. Per 100g: 234 kcal, 5.1g protein, 13.7g fat (8.6g saturated), 18.6g carbohydrate, 0.7g fibre, 200mg sodium. Per plate (350g): ~820 kcal, ~18g protein, ~48g fat (~30g saturated fat = 150% of WHO daily saturated fat limit), ~65g carbohydrate, ~700mg sodium. GI ~52 (low, fettuccine al dente), GL ~34 per plate (high). The dish is vegetarian and halal. Clinically, the primary concern is the extraordinary saturated fat load (30g/plate), which is the highest of any common Western restaurant pasta dish and poses significant cardiovascular risk with regular consumption. The high GL also makes it unsuitable for T2DM patients at full portion. Sodium is moderate relative to cured-meat pasta dishes. The dish originated in Rome (~1908, Alfredo di Lelio) and has been widely adopted in Singapore's Italian restaurant scene.
common_user_queries: how much saturated fat in cream pasta
is alfredo pasta good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA SR Legacy ingredient data rather than direct laboratory analysis of a Singapore restaurant Alfredo dish. Exact cream-to-butter ratios and parmesan quantities vary between restaurants, introducing moderate uncertainty in fat and saturated fat values. GI is an analogous estimate from published fettuccine al dente data. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Alfredo dish (fettuccine + butter + double cream + parmesan) is not directly available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central; individual ingredient fatty acid data could be used for a future component-level calculation but has not been performed in this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: Fettuccine Alfredo contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients (no vegetables, no citrus); this is naturally zero for this preparation. trans_fat_g of 0.1 per 100g reflects trace naturally-occurring trans fats from dairy (conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid in butter and cream), consistent with USDA SR Legacy dairy component data. Saturated fat per plate (~30g) represents approximately 150% of the WHO recommended daily maximum of 20g saturated fat. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 65) / 100 = 33.8. Source: component calculation USDA SR Legacy, accessed 2026-04-14. q8 fix (iteration 3): self-check performed on aliases_by_language vs flat aliases array — all entries in aliases_by_language['english'], ['chinese'], ['malay'], ['tamil'], ['hindi'], and ['romanized'] verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. 'pasta krim mentega' confirmed present in flat aliases. All 17 flat alias strings accounted for.
